text: We use a 15mm blade and a mosquito forceps to enter into the pleural space with damp dissection.
A 5mm 30 degree angle camera
is advanced and CO2 is insufflated until lung collapse is achieved.
It is also helpful to use a left bronchus blocker.
We carefully inspect the other port entry.
A left-handed grasper and a right-handed dissector are used to open the mediastinal pleura from the left suclavian artery to descending aorta.
It is important to seal thoroughly the lymphatic vessels over the subclavian artery with cautery.
The crossing vein is dissected and divided
with ligasure Maryland.
This allows a better exposition and dissection of the descending aorta and ligamentum arteriosum
The left suclavian artery is not in continuity with the ductal ligament, even if it looks like at a glance.

The left suclavian artery is part of the first brachiocephalic trunk, but the tension of the ductal ligament that connects the descending aorta with the pulmonary artery approaches the left suclavian artery.
We have to be very careful not to damage the recurrent laryngeal nerve that surrounds the ligamentum arteriosum.
A vessel loop is used to mobilize the ligamentum.
The endopinut allows us to perform a non-traumatic dissection.
We ligate the ligamentum with hemlock clips.
This non-absorbable polymer clip has a lock engagement feature as well as teeth in the jaws that provide good security.
It is vital to control the clip closing mechanism at all times so as not to inadvertently catch other structures and ensure total ligament occlusion.
It is not necessary to take out the applicator to load it.
It is automatically loaded by pressing the applicator.
15 clips are available.
The Emolux have been tested to withstand supraphysiological pressure.
There are four sizes of Emolock clips and appliers from 2 to 16 mm.
Clips ligate up to 10 mm of tissue through a 5 mm stroker and up to 16 mm through a 10 mm stroker.
They are compatible with CT scan and MRI.
Four Emolocks are used.
ligament is cut, leaving two hemologs on each side.
Although preparative tests showed a non-painting ductus, it is better to proceed with calcium in this step.
The final step
is to divide adhesive bands surrounding the esophagus and trachea.
It is an important part of the procedure that helps the two structures to become wide apart.
We check that there is no lymphatic leakage.
Tissue cold is applied to seal the lymphatic vessels securely.
The mediastinal pleura is left widely open.
